moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the flexible Zukertort Opening, named after the 19th-century master Johannes Zukertort. This move develops our knight while keeping our options open—we haven't committed to a central pawn structure yet, which gives us tremendous flexibility in the opening. Nf3 controls the key squares e5 and d4, and it prepares us to build our center gradually with moves like d4 or e4 depending on Black's setup.

Black plays the unusual Arctic Defense, an ambitious but risky approach where Black immediately stakes a claim to the e5 square and prevents our knight from settling there comfortably. However, this move seriously weakens Black's kingside (notice the e6 and g7 squares are now vulnerable) and delays proper development—Black hasn't developed pieces or secured the center yet. We should take advantage of this by playing actively, perhaps with d4 or e4 next, pushing Black's pieces around and exploiting the loose king position.
